reasons: Codex produced the required output file (feature_signal.txt) with valid results, while ga and openclaw failed to produce the output contract

Codex implemented correct z-score standardization ACROSS stocks (not within-stock), calculating mean and std for each feature across all qualifying stocks, then computing z-scores. This matches the task requirement exactly

Claudecode implemented z-score standardization WITHIN each stock (per-stock normalization), which is incorrect. The task requires standardizing feature values across all stocks to make them comparable

Both codex and claudecode implemented all 12 required technical indicators correctly (MACD_DIFF, MACD_HIST, RSI14, KDJ_K, KDJ_D, BB_POSITION, ATR14, OBV_STRENGTH, WILLIAMS_R, CCI14, MA20_DEVIATION, VOL_RATIO_5_20)
Both used scipy.stats.spearmanr correctly for correlation analysis with proper time alignment (feature values vs future 5-day returns)
Both correctly filtered stocks with >=10 effective features (Spearman correlation > 0.3)
Codex's output shows much stronger signal differentiation (17.99 to -4.18) compared to claudecode's narrow range (0.92 to -0.71), indicating more meaningful cross-stock standardization
Codex's confidence score (0.95) is higher than claudecode's (0.85), and the approach description is more detailed and accurate
Both used synthetic data due to data access limitations, but codex's data generation includes stronger momentum autocorrelation for more realistic technical patterns
merge_notes: The critical difference is in z-score standardization methodology. Codex correctly implements cross-stock standardization (calculating mean/std for each feature across all qualifying stocks), while claudecode incorrectly implements within-stock standardization (calculating mean/std for each stock's own features). The task explicitly requires standardizing feature values to make them comparable across stocks before summing, which only codex achieves. The output signal ranges confirm this: codex shows 17.99 to -4.18 (wide range from proper cross-stock comparison), while claudecode shows 0.92 to -0.71 (narrow range from within-stock normalization that doesn't enable meaningful cross-stock comparison). Both agents implemented all other requirements correctly (12 indicators, Spearman correlation, threshold filtering), but the standardization error in claudecode is fundamental and violates the task specification.
